findings.summary: Supabase client bootstrap is a high-blast-radius hub with mixed runtime responsibilities
evidence: The main client module wires instrumentation, auth state wiring, reconnect policy, and singleton lifecycle in one entrypoint.
Changes to auth, realtime, or instrumentation behavior all converge through the same bootstrap file, increasing change coupling.
findings.suggestion: Split client bootstrap into narrow initializers (auth bootstrap, realtime bootstrap, instrumentation bootstrap) and keep client.ts as a thin composition layer with explicit startup order.
findings.reasoning: Reducing bootstrap coupling lowers regression risk and makes runtime ownership boundaries clearer.

findings.summary: Core shared-lib modules are overloaded and act as cross-domain utility hubs
evidence: errorHandler.ts, utils.ts, and queryKeys.ts are each imported broadly across unrelated feature areas.
High import concentration indicates these modules are carrying multiple concerns and become default dumping grounds.
findings.suggestion: Split shared/lib by capability (error transport vs user messaging, string/file helpers vs UI helpers, query key families by domain) and introduce narrower module boundaries with explicit ownership.
findings.reasoning: More focused abstractions improve locality, reduce accidental dependencies, and make refactors safer.

findings.summary: UI primitive layer shows convention drift in variant naming and styling strategy
evidence: Button variants include legacy aliases and multiple style dialects in one primitive component.
Sibling UI primitives mix different API/styling conventions, creating local style islands.
findings.suggestion: Define a small, enforced UI-primitive contract (variant taxonomy, naming rules, deprecation window), migrate legacy aliases behind a compatibility shim, and align sibling primitives to the same contract.
findings.reasoning: Consistent primitive conventions reduce downstream UI entropy and simplify design-system evolution.
